coaching_report:
# Coaching Report — Tuesday Strength (tue_strength A) | 2026-03-17

**Solid execution overall, Nayan.** Full compliance on every prescribed element — all 15 working sets completed across the five exercises, the full 30-minute Hip Complex mobility block done, and the session came in right at the target duration of 63.7 minutes. That discipline matters, especially on a strength day that sits inside a broader triathlon load week. The hip flexor fatigue you're feeling from the mobility work is exactly the right kind of productive discomfort — those tissues take a long time to adapt, so consistent exposure like this will pay dividends in your run stride length and bike hip position over the coming weeks.

**On the load side, the 8kg kettlebell is a starting point worth reviewing.** For context, your FTP of 190W indicates a reasonably well-developed neuromuscular base, and 8kg across all five movements — including the KB Swing and Goblet Squat — sits at the lighter end of what will drive meaningful posterior chain and single-leg strength adaptation. The RPE 7 is useful data: that's an appropriate target for this session type, but I'd want to understand *where* that RPE was coming from. If it was primarily from the hip mobility work and the cumulative volume rather than from muscular load on the swings and goblet squats specifically, then the kettlebell weight likely has room to go up. For the Single-leg RDL, 8kg at RPE 7 is more defensible — control and balance are genuinely taxing at lower loads — and your coaching cue note about hamstring tension and control suggests you were executing that one well.

**For the next session, the priority is a load progression on the swing and goblet squat.** Try moving to 12kg on those two movements while keeping 8kg on the Single-leg RDL until balance is rock solid, and maintaining whatever feels right on the Suitcase Carry and Row. Keep the 60–90 second rest windows honest — with a heavier swing especially, it's tempting to rush. The hip work is clearly landing well, so no changes there. Keep stacking it.

coaching_report:
# Coaching Report — Thursday Strength (B) | 2026-03-19

**Overall:** Solid effort getting this session done, Nayan. A compliance score of 75 tells a reasonable story — the session happened, the full 30 minutes of T-spine mobility was completed (that's the piece most athletes quietly skip), and an RPE of 6 on a strength B session is right in the target window. At your training load — FTP 190W, LTHR 165 — this strength work is serving a specific purpose: building the hip stability and anti-rotation control that keep your run economy and bike position honest in the back half of a race. Showing up and executing it at moderate intensity without digging a recovery hole is exactly the intent.

**What went well:** The TGUs and KB Cleans with an 8 kg bell were an appropriate choice given where you are in building this pattern — slow and deliberate is the entire point of the TGU, so if you held the 45–60 second per rep tempo, the load is secondary to the movement quality at this stage. Full mobility block completed is a genuine win and worth noting: T-spine restriction is one of the quieter limiters for triathlete aero position and shoulder rotation in the water, so consistency here compounds over weeks. Your request to add neck tucks is noted and well-timed — we can slot those into the mobility block rather than the strength circuit, which keeps the movement intent clean.

**What to improve:** Two things need attention before next Thursday. First, the Bulgarian Split Squat stability issue is useful diagnostic information — dropping to bodyweight is a fine short-term call, but the likely culprit is rear-foot placement and hip position rather than raw strength. Next session, try the rear foot on a lower surface (a single plate or a low step rather than a bench) and focus on sinking straight down rather than forward; once the pattern clicks, load will follow quickly. Second, the Renegade Row being "spotty" almost certainly means hip rotation was creeping in under fatigue — this is the one exercise in this session with the most direct carryover to your running posture and swim catch, so it earns stricter attention. Drop to your knees if needed to own the hips-square position for all reps before loading it further; a clean 3×5 kneeling beats a sloppy 3×5 standing every time.

coaching_report:
# Coaching Report — Thursday Swim | 2026-03-19

**Athlete:** Nayan | **Session type:** Unplanned (rescheduled Wednesday session)

---

Good to have the context, Nayan — glad you got the Wednesday session in, even if it shifted a day. Based on your reply, this was a 59-minute swim covering 930m, which works out to an average pace of roughly 378s/100m. To put that in perspective, your CSS benchmark is 180s/100m, so this session was sitting well below aerobic intensity from a pure pace standpoint — likely a relaxed, social effort given you were swimming with your girlfriend. That's completely fine as a recovery-adjacent or lifestyle swim, but it's worth being clear-eyed that it doesn't replace the stimulus the Wednesday session was originally designed to deliver. The volume (930m) is also on the lighter side for a structured aerobic swim block.

What went well here is simple: you showed up. Life happens, schedules shift, and getting in the water at all — even a day late and at an easy pace — keeps your feel for the water, maintains routine, and is far better than a zero. There's genuine value in low-intensity aquatic time, particularly if your body needed the easier effort after mid-week training load. The fact that you communicated the context proactively is also exactly the kind of feedback loop that makes coaching work.

That said, the Wednesday prescription has effectively gone unexecuted at its intended intensity, and we now have a small compliance gap building. Given this session was unplanned and below threshold, it doesn't count toward your structured swim volume for the week. **Would you be open to slotting a short, focused 30–40 minute quality swim this weekend — something like a CSS-based main set of 6×100m at your 180s/100m target — to make sure you're hitting the aerobic adaptation we were after this week before we move into next week's load?**
